data.title: Dutch Bros: Growth Remains Solid, But Shares Look Priced For FY2028
data.description:
Dutch Bros (BROS) is a rapidly growing drive-through coffee chain company. A double-digit top- and bottom-line growth story has lost its positive price momentum, indicating a 1% drop in stock price year-to-date.

Although the recent sell-off may appear appealing, the company trades at a 75x earnings multiple, representing a 4.6x premium valuation versus peers at 16x. Even if the company meets Wall Street estimates and reaches $1.60 in diluted EPS in FY2028, this would put the stock at a 32x earnings multiple, about a 2x premium versus the sector median.

This argues that the stock is likely to consolidate further. Unless the company unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, it seems the market may have priced in future growth in the stock price. This leaves little room for error for management, although its performance remains outstanding.

Therefore, I rate Dutch Bros a "Hold" with a $53 price target over the next year. This suggests a 4% upside possibility, yet further consolidation. I don't think the company will significantly outperform the benchmark in the near term, unless a substantial catalyst emerges.

FQ3 2025: Steady Top And Bottom Line Performance

On Nov. 5, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was the 9th consecutive double-beat quarter, highlighting solid top- and bottom-line growth. However, the market reacted to the results with a 4% decline over the following trading day.

Dutch Bros posted roughly $424 million in revenue, a 25% increase on a year-over-year basis, and $10 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $0.19 in diluted EPS, a double-digit surge versus $0.16 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.02 above analysts' estimates. Although the market reaction may appear surprising to a double-beat performance, an overly inflated multiple could be the reason behind the breather.

In the earnings presentation, Dutch Bros highlighted that it has achieved 14% growth in the company's operated shops, an increase from 645 to 759 on a year-over-year basis, and an 18% surge in franchised shops, reaching 322 versus 305 in the same quarter last year. The total number of coffee chain shops reached 1,081. The company is strengthening its brand, market position, and positioning for future growth.

On the downside, the company's operated shop margin has decreased over the past 12 months. It has declined from 29.5% to 27.8%, and this remains an area to monitor for long-term investors. If margins were to erode further, I don't think the market will be willing to pay a significant premium for the company's operations. This could also result in weaker-than-anticipated bottom-line performance, weighing on the stock price and sentiment.

On the upside, the company managed to increase company-operated same-shop sales by 7.4%, which appears to indicate a solid demand. This also implies that the company's top line is mainly driven by openings of the new coffee chains, yet solid mid-single-digit growth suggests a strong market position and consumer preferences favoring BROS' coffee shops. The systemwide same-shop sales rose by 5.7%.

To conclude, this was a solid quarterly performance for the company, indicating that the business is running on all cylinders. However, although the company managed to achieve a double-digit top- and bottom-line growth, there are some concerning areas to highlight.

The company's margins are eroding, and I believe this is mainly impacted by cost inflation tied to input and labor. In addition to this, although mid-single-digit top-line growth in terms of same-store sales may appear compelling, I am not sure whether this is sufficient to maintain such a premium valuation. If the economy enters a recession, there is a possibility of weakening performance; therefore, I think investors should remain cautious at these levels.

Dutch Bros Trades At A Premium Multiple; I Favor Consolidation

Dutch Bros trades at a forward P/E of approximately 75x, highlighting a 4.6x overvaluation versus peers at 16x. Although the chart below indicates that the coffee chain is used to trading at these levels, this remains a substantial premium, and I am not sure whether the company's performance justifies such an elevated valuation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 2.33, the company appears to be trading at approximately a 43% premium to peers at 1.63. This supports a bearish stance. Moreover, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 4.66 and 4.06, respectively, highlight a 3.5x overvaluation at the midpoint versus sector medians of 1.26 and 0.87.

This is important due to a few things. To begin with, the valuation metrics suggest that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth. This means that if BROS decelerates top-line performance or even maintains a high double-digit revenue growth pace, it may not necessarily translate to significant stock price appreciation. Furthermore, this argues for a significant premium, and if sentiment changes in the market, Dutch Bros would likely correct to closer-to-market valuations. It does not mean the stock can't trade at these levels, but investors should remain cautious.

The company has a moderately leveraged capital structure, implying $1.04 billion in total obligations and $268 million in cash. This resulted in $27.6 million in interest expenses on a TTM basis, which remains an elevated financial burden. This may partially cloud the positive outlook, as interest expenses weigh on profitability and bottom-line growth.

A significant top- and bottom-line expansion remains a key trait fueling a bullish narrative. Over the past year, Dutch Bros achieved a 29% growth on a year-over-year basis, representing a 9x outperformance versus peers at 3%. The bottom line grew by nearly 57%, an impressive advantage versus the sector median of only 1% growth. This supports a positive outlook and justifies the company trading at a high valuation. However, based on the recent quarterly performance, comparable sales grew at a mid-single-digit pace, arguing that growth is mainly driven by newly opened coffee chains. If BROS reduces the expansion rate, it may meaningfully affect top-line growth; thus, this should remain an area to monitor.

On the downside, Dutch Bros operates at below-market margins, and I think that heavily contributes to the bearish narrative. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 26% suggests a 32% underperformance versus peers at 39%. The net income margin (TTM) of 4.04% implies a 7% disadvantage versus the sector median of 4.34%. I understand that the company remains top-line focused, driven by double-digit expansion and openings of new coffee shops, but this may weigh on the stock price.

Dutch Bros may appear to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors seeking to diversify into a high-growth consumer discretionary name. The coffee chain company delivers a rapid, double-digit top and bottom line, continues opening new locations, and is franchising the brand. However, it also operates at below-market margins, remains moderately leveraged, and trades at a significant premium, which may cap future performance. This results in a mixed picture, thus leading to my Hold rating.

Wall Street estimates $0.68 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $0.89 in diluted EPS for FY2026. This would present a continuation of a solid uptrend in the bottom-line performance. If the economy doesn't enter a recession, Dutch Bros appears to be well-positioned to achieve these targets. However, I think the stock may consolidate for a while, resulting in decreased earnings multiple.

Therefore, if we apply a 60x earnings multiple to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $0.89, we arrive at my price target of $53. This would represent about a 10% lower valuation due to consolidation, yet stock price appreciation is fueled by bottom-line growth. Nevertheless, this would argue for a 4% increase in the stock price over the next year, as I think investors may favor less expensive alternatives in the space. Although a higher surge in the stock price is possible, I am not sure whether Dutch Bros may experience a significant increase in multiple expansions, leading to the benchmark outperformance, without unlocking a new top- and bottom-line growth catalyst.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Dutch Bros that may impact the stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company appears to be trading at a significant premium to both peers and the average company in the S&P 500. If sentiment significantly shifts or the economy enters a recession, Dutch Bros may correct. I doubt that the downside is protected, and BROS is not likely to sustain such a premium valuation if sentiment dramatically shifts. Based on my analysis, it also appears that the market may have run ahead of the company's fundamentals. It leaves little room for error.

The consumer discretionary industry is fierce, especially within the coffee shop landscape. There are multiple companies competing for the market share, such as Starbucks, and it is challenging to have a sticky customer base. If consumer preferences change, or other coffee shops attract BROS's customers, top-line growth may decelerate, and it could be challenging to justify the company's premium valuation. Furthermore, if inflation persists or consumer spending declines, the non-essential services may experience reduced demand. This would not be a favorable environment for consumer discretionary companies.

On the other hand, if none of the risks and concerns materialize, Dutch Bros may continue expanding its business. If it succeeds in maintaining a double-digit top and bottom line over the following quarters, investors may be willing to maintain an elevated earnings multiple.

Conclusion: Dutch Bros Appears To Be Too Expensive To Argue For A Significant Multiple Expansion

I think that although Dutch Bros may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking a high-growth consumer discretionary name, at the current valuation, it is difficult to justify multiple expansion. The coffee company trades at roughly 4.6x overvaluation to peers, and that remains significant.

Dutch Bros experiences substantial demand, but the top-line growth is mainly driven by newly opened company-operated shops and franchised shops. The same-store sales achieved a mid-single-digit growth pace, and I am not sure whether it would be enough to maintain such an elevated premium if the macroeconomic outlook weakens. I think the company is likely to consolidate unless it unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, which boosts the stock higher.

Therefore, I rate Dutch Bros a "Hold" with a $53 price target over the next 12 months. This suggests 4% upside potential, but also broader market underperformance. 19 Wall Street analysts imply a $76 price target over the next year, suggesting a 51% upside possibility. Although BROS could achieve the target, I am not feeling that bullish. I believe the company is more likely to consolidate.

data.title: The Fed Is Unlikely To Lower Interest Rates In The December FOMC Meeting
data.description:
This week, the market was highly anticipating two key events. The first one was Nvidia's Q3 FY26 results. In my pre-earnings article on Nvidia, I was bullish going into earnings, although I warned that, similar to AMD, a beat and raise could not lead to an immediate bull run.

Well, it turns out that was the case, as the market sold the rip on Thursday.

In fact, compared to the closing on Wednesday, the closing price on Thursday was down 3%.

The other event was the release of the employment situation report for September by the BLS. As I initially anticipated in my last macro article, the unemployment rate ticked up (4.4%), even though the (nonfarm) payrolls came in above expectations.

From a fundamentals perspective, I have a high conviction that AI stocks have not deteriorated. Earnings are beating expectations, and growth figures are well in the double digits for both hyperscalers and neocloud players. I see the selloff as an overreaction. That said, I don't see any signs that suggest an immediate rebound to all-time highs. Below, I explain why.

Circularity Is Not Fake Demand

Let me first explain why I believe the markets are selling off despite AI players in the likes of Palantir, Nvidia, AMD, Microsoft, or Nebius obliterating the Street's top-line expectations.

On October 4, Bloomberg published an article titled: "Why Fears of a Trillion-Dollar AI Bubble Are Growing". Since then, I noticed two main trends:

First, the proliferation of the term AI bubble in the titles of many articles from major media outlets and even in the macro section of Seeking Alpha. Second, the chart below:

First, circularity in financing and partnerships doesn’t mean revenues are fake. Back in the 2010s, AWS, Microsoft, and Google all sold infrastructure to each other and to overlapping customers. In the earlier smartphone cycle, Apple, Qualcomm, and many app developers were all tightly intertwined. To me, the key question is whether there is end-user demand outside the loop?

Well, take a look at the chart below from a McKinsey study, particularly at the light blue line representing the usage of generative AI among businesses in the US.

The same report shows in which department AI adoption is taking place: technology, especially software engineering. If you have ever used Cursor or CodEx for vibe coding, you know by now how useful AI is to generate pretty much any app that you want with zero experience in coding (as a startup founder, I'm speaking from my own experience here).

Another factor that suggests strong AI adoption is the recent wave of layoffs from big tech firms. The Challenger, Gray & Christmas report cited artificial intelligence as a reason for the 48,400 job cuts for 2025. I argue that, as more corporations wake up (three years after the start of the AI boom), we could see this figure accelerating in the next years, especially in publicly traded companies, where the need to maximize the bottom line results is key in each quarter. The most notable recent wave of layoffs was at Amazon, after the company decided to cut 14,000 corporate roles while simultaneously ramping up AI investment.

Finally, the last earnings season was strong, especially in the information technology sector.

Once again, the sectors where AI is not easily implemented (consumer defensive, consumer discretionary, energy, or health care) are showing a significant underperformance compared to the sectors where there is AI traction (mainly the technology sector).

The sequential increase in EPS growth from Q2 to Q3 this year was 5 percentage points for the tech sector. If that figure doesn't show growth, you tell me what does in the comment section.

We Haven't Hit The Lows Yet: The Fed Meeting Could Be A Turning Point

Price action and fundamentals are not the same thing. That's applicable both on the upside (when price action is ahead of fundamentals) and on the downside (like now, when fundamentals are strong, but price action is not following through).

As shown above, a strong uptrend was broken yesterday. The next support level is 6% below today's level in the S&P 500.

From a sentiment perspective, I see a lot of bearishness.

I believe a good part of this downward pressure is driven by the low expectations of another rate cut from the Fed. The BLS will not release the October employment situation report, and the one for November was delayed to December 16, 2025. Normally, jobs reports are on the first Friday of the month, but this schedule has been altered because of the shutdown delays.

A few days ago, Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin emphasized that the Fed is operating with limited data, seeing inflation still above target and job growth slowing down, and that upcoming data will guide the decision for the December meeting. According to the CMS Group, the chance for another rate cut has drastically declined in the past month, now significantly below 50%.

Conclusion

I believe the disappointment after the BLS delayed the November payrolls report has led to increased concerns of a further rate cut in the December 10 FOMC meeting.

The Fed only has the September payroll data, which came in above expectations. That said, the unemployment rate in September increased 10 bps from August, which is the only positive spin that I can make of this situation.

With no October jobs report and November delayed after the FOMC meeting, I believe the downward pressure could persist until December 10 (at least). Based on price action alone, there is a 6% downward move until the next support in the S&P 500.

Given that the spectacular earnings from Nvidia (and guidance for FQ4) were not enough to lift the sentiment, I foresee a continuation of the recent sell-off in the broader markets.

The next FOMC meeting on December 10 could be a catalyst to reignite optimism. However, the chance for another rate cut has decreased substantially due to the delay of the November payrolls report. If the Fed doesn't cut rates in December, I believe we could be in for an unpleasant surprise. For now, I downgrade to a Hold, and I recommend monitoring the Consumer Price Index report, which will be released on December 10, 2025, at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time (right before the FOMC meeting, usually at 2pm ET). If inflation spikes up, the chance for a rate cut could decrease substantially.

data.title: Comfort Systems USA: Shares Have Gotten Too Hot
data.description:
Back in August of this year, I decided to revisit Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX). In that article, I argued that while shares of the company were anything but cheap, they were cheap enough, given the rapid growth that the firm has seen in recent years, to justify a bullish outlook. My decision at that time to reaffirm the business as a "Buy" candidate played out well, with the stock now up 29.6% since then. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up only 4.5%. But that's nothing. Since I first rated the company a "Buy" in August 2023, shares have skyrocketed 418.1%. That's massively above the 49.3% increase that the broader market saw. In all likelihood, revenue, profits, and cash flows will continue to grow in the coming years. But at some point, shares are no longer attractively priced. Unfortunately, I think that moment is now. Because of its valuation, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

Shares Have Gotten A Little Too Hot

The only new data that we have regarding Comfort Systems USA covers the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was a whopping $2.45 billion. That's an increase of 35.2% over the $1.81 billion the company reported a year earlier. To really understand why revenue grew so much, we need to dig into its different operating segments. The largest of these, by a significant margin, is its Mechanical segment, which involves its HVAC, plumbing, piping and controls, and off-site construction, monitoring, and fire protection offerings. Then we have the Electrical segment, which focuses more on the installation and servicing of electrical systems.

Year over year, the Mechanical segment for the company saw revenue jump 25.8%, from $1.44 billion to $1.81 billion. Management attributed this increase mostly to the same store activity that contributed $336.6 million of the top-line increase for the unit. This was thanks to stronger demand in the technology sector that positively affected its operations in North Carolina as well as Texas. However, a small bit of the increase in revenue, $35.2 million in total, came from its acquisitions of Right Way Plumbing & Mechanical and Century Contractors. In May of this year, the company purchased Right Way in a deal valued at $65 million. And in January, it acquired Century Contractors, a self-performing mechanical installation, pipe fabrication and installation, steel erection, equipment setting, and concrete installation business. That purchase cost shareholders $84.2 million.

Even more impressive was the Electrical segment. Revenue there skyrocketed 71.4%, from $373.7 million to $640.5 million. This surge in revenue is thanks mostly to the technology sector. Management said that higher activity at its Texas electrical operation boosted sales by $200.3 million. With this increase came a rise in profitability. Overall operating income for this unit went from $50.4 million to $108.2 million. Having said that, the Mechanical segment for the company was no slouch, with profitability growing nicely from $165.5 million to $287.8 million.

I already mentioned technology here. And sure enough, technology is the big driver for the business. From the third quarter of last year to the third quarter of this year, technology revenue shot up from $615.4 million to $1.12 billion. That's a near doubling. It appears as though data centers and, to a lesser extent, chip plants, are major sources of this revenue growth. Management even mentioned that data centers are more compelling than manufacturing opportunities. This is not to imply that there's no growth elsewhere. Manufacturing revenue grew from $452.2 million to $543.1 million. We also saw growth in health care, office buildings, the government side of things, and multifamily and residential family areas. The only weak spots ended up being education, retail, restaurants, and entertainment facilities, and other miscellaneous revenue sources.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability shot up. Net income expanded from $146.2 million to $291.6 million. That's an approximate doubling. Operating cash flow skyrocketed from $302.2 million to $553.3 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $211.8 million to $358.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business jumped from $238.3 million to $413.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows are all rising materially. Clearly, this is evidence that this is not just a blip on the radar, but is part of a larger trend. Normally, when you see such strong growth, there is the risk that backlog might be eroded, leading to lower future sales. But that's not the case here. In the chart below, you can see how backlog has increased over time. Currently, it's at an all-time high of $9.38 billion.

There are some other metrics that we can use to evaluate the company. The first would be the number of projects that the business has. The firm ended the third quarter with 8,974 projects that it has contracted for. That's up from the 8,811 projects it reported a year earlier. And as the number of projects increased, the total contract value that the company has locked in has shot up also, growing from $14.72 billion last year to $21.17 billion this year.

In all likelihood, this kind of stellar growth will continue. After all, the data center side of the business is looking great. Management specifically mentioned that the pipeline for the company remains strong. According to one source, data center demand is expected to grow materially over the next few years as AI fuels cloud revenue to $2 trillion by the end of this decade. That compares to the $496 billion that we saw last year. And according to consulting giant McKinsey & Company, it's believed that the world will need about $6.7 trillion in spending in this space to meet AI demand. Certainly, Comfort Systems USA will be a beneficiary of this.

Having said that, I have found it to become increasingly difficult in recent days to be bullish about the business. Even though I fully expect revenue, profits, and cash flows to continue growing, shares are no longer as cheap as they were previously. If we project our results based on what we have seen already this year, we would expect net income for 2025 of $959.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $1.20 billion, and EBITDA of $1.40 billion.

If we take these estimates and the historical results that the company saw last year, then in the chart above we can see how the company is currently priced. Although the trading multiple has come down (year-over-year) significantly because of recent growth, the fact of the matter is that these are rather lofty multiples on an absolute basis. Relative to other similar companies, I would argue that shares are expensive as well. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. What I found was that, while on a price-to-earnings basis only two of the five firms were cheaper than it is, that does not hold when we look at the other two trading multiples. On an EV-to-EBITDA basis, four of the five firms that I stacked it up against are cheaper than it is. And on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, our candidate is the most expensive of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Comfort Systems USA 32.9 26.4 22.0
EMCOR Group (EME) 24.9 22.5 16.8
AECOM (ACM) 27.2 19.4 16.2
Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) 32.0 23.7 27.2
MasTec (MTZ) 45.2 23.2 18.1
Stantec (STN) 38.1 23.9 18.6

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, Comfort Systems USA is a really great business. The company has a net cash position of $724.5 million. Growth lately has been strong, and backlog expansion suggests additional growth moving forward. Long term, I suspect that the company will do fine. Having said that, I do have some concerns. For starters, I think the US economy is heading toward a recession. In the most recent article, where I had touched on that topic, I also pointed out that the euphoria centered around AI looks very much like a bubble to me. And then on top of that, we have the firm's valuation. After shooting up so much since August, the stock is looking increasingly more expensive. And even though I won't go so far as to turn bearish on it at this time, I do think that downgrading it to a "Hold" would be appropriate.

data.title: The Fed's Made A Big Mistake
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Markets have seen a historic selloff in the last two weeks, and there’s one main culprit.

After laying out a clear path for rate cuts back in November, it seems like the Federal Reserve has now turned uncharacteristically hawkish.

Citing concerns over inflation and poor visibility, it seems like we may not even get a rate cut in December.

Together with liquidity strains in the Repo market, the overall liquidity picture has noticeably worsened, and this may be the true reason behind this decline.

But even if we see a continued selloff, the opportunity to go long here is extremely compelling in my opinion.

The Fed’s Hawkish Turn: A Major Policy Misstep

The Fed’s recent messaging represents one of the clearest policy pivots we’ve seen all year, and arguably, one of its biggest mistakes.

Only weeks ago, the central bank signaled a fairly predictable glide path: two cuts delivered, more easing likely, and inflation progressing steadily toward target.

Markets relaxed, liquidity picked up, and risk assets surged. But suddenly, a chorus of FOMC members began warning about “persistent inflation risks,” “insufficient labor market cooling,” and the need for a “high bar to additional easing.”

This would normally be standard Fed posturing, but the timing here is terrible.

Economic data isn’t breaking, and inflation isn’t re-accelerating.

Labor market dynamics aren’t spiraling. If anything, the delayed September jobs report, which showed 119,000 jobs added with unemployment rising to 4.2%, suggests disinflation is doing exactly what the Fed wants.

So why the hawkish tone?

Several regional presidents, Schmid, Logan, Hammack, Collins, and Kashkari, went on a coordinated media tour arguing that inflation remains “too hot,” even acknowledging that some of the recent upside is tariff-driven and therefore outside monetary policy’s control.

The October FOMC minutes reinforced this shift, revealing that many members were never fully on board with the first two cuts and that a December reduction is far from guaranteed. Powell, who had been leaning dovish, has now adopted a more “wait-and-see” tone.

This pivot, coming so late in the easing cycle and right when financial conditions were stabilizing, has created, in my opinion, unnecessary volatility.

Liquidity Is the Real Story

When risk assets drop 2–4% in hours, when Bitcoin (BTC-USD) falls 30% in a month, and when $1.5 trillion is wiped from the S&P 500 (SP500) in a single day, you know something bigger than “slightly hawkish Fed speeches” is happening.

The real issue is liquidity, which has already been teetering.

The repo market has seen meaningful strains, with SOFR volatility and pockets of funding stress suggesting collateral scarcity and year-end balance sheet pressures are building faster than anticipated.

Quantitative Tightening, or QT, continues draining reserves at an aggressive pace. Over $1.3 trillion in QT this cycle has taken the system toward the lower bound of “ample” reserves.

CME FedWatch probabilities for a December cut collapsed from 70–95% to around 43%.

Treasury issuance remains heavy, even though post-election issuance slowed slightly; the sheer volume of supply combined with reduced Fed/TGA liquidity puts upward pressure on yields.

Add these pieces together and you get the selloff we’re witnessing.

The way I see it, this is a mechanical unwind caused by liquidity tightening at the exact moment markets expected easing.

Add to this the recent issues with Japan, and you have a recipe for disaster.

Why This Sell-Off Looks Like an Opportunity

If the Fed continues tightening financial conditions through rhetoric alone, the market will eventually force its hand.

This is not 2022. We are not fighting entrenched inflation, and we are not dealing with overheating labor markets.

We are in a structurally strong economy with:

  • 2.8% GDP growth.

  • Disinflationary trends intact.

  • Cooling labor pressures.

  • A historically resilient corporate earnings cycle.

  • A multi-trillion-dollar AI capex boom.

This is the wrong time for hawkish overcorrection.

And that’s precisely why it won’t last.

The Treasury does not want a disorderly tightening. The Fed, or at least the current administration, does not want instability heading into 2026.

If liquidity strains worsen, the response is predictable:

  • Return to Quantitative Easing ("QE").

  • Adjustments to the RRP facility.

  • More measured forward guidance.

  • A renewed push for earlier cuts.

The Fed has always stepped in when the liquidity plumbing starts to creak. This time will be no different.

Final Thoughts

This sell-off feels violent because of the sheer amount of leverage that had built up across equities, crypto, and derivatives. But we need to separate price action from fundamentals.

Fundamentally speaking, nothing has broken; inflation isn’t spirallng. AI-driven investment isn’t slowing, and corporate balance sheets remain strong.

What has changed is the Fed’s tone, and that is fixable.

When a central bank makes a messaging error, markets reprice quickly and brutally. But those repricings often create the best buying opportunities.

If December’s data softens even modestly, if the repo market shows further strain, the Fed will be pushed back onto the easing track.

And when that pivot happens, today’s levels will look like a gift.

data.title: Oracle Stock Plunges 40%: Why This Could Be The Best Buying Opportunity Yet
data.description:
For a fleeting moment, Oracle Corporation (ORCL) Founder Larry Ellison was the world’s richest person. That came on September 10, 2025, shortly after the Systems Software industry company reported quarterly results that blew away analysts' expectations. Shares soared, prompting pundits to wonder if a new member of the trillion-dollar-market-cap club was all but a foregone certainty.

But that day marked an epic top in the now $600 billion market cap Information Technology-sector stock. The firm took on immense debt loads, pushing its debt-to-equity ratio to 500% (a bit less if you back out the cash). What’s more, credit default swaps on Oracle bonds have jumped to more than one percent (to insure 5-year Oracle paper). It has shades of the 2008 banking crisis, when traders would bid up CDS to spook the equity market.

But I reiterate a buy rating on Oracle Corporation (ORCL). I was bullish on the stock back in April, and even with the more than 40% plunge since mid-September, shares are up 48% since that analysis, more than double the S&P 500’s (SP500) return. Today, I’ll note a favorable valuation amid the carnage and volatility, along with why the chart points to a bullish reversal.

Oracle CDS Price Rising

Risk: Oracle Debt To Equity Ratio Hits 500%

Back in September, Oracle reported a soft set of quarterly results on the surface. Its fiscal Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.47 missed the Wall Street consensus forecast by a penny, while revenue of $14.9 billion, up 12% from the same period a year earlier, was a small $110 million miss. Q1 cloud net sales grew 28% YoY, while its Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) revenue rose 55%.

Shares soared 37% in the session that followed, its best single-day rally since 1993. Ahead of its Q2 report due out on December 8th, the options market prices in a very high 9.8% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the reporting date. That’s the most expensive options pricing in at least the last three years, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the slight top and bottom-line misses were more than overshadowed by rapid growth in Oracle’s RPO/backlog growth of 230% from the end of its Q4 (+359% YoY). The company took bold steps to enter the AI arms race and emerged as a key cloud AI hyperscaler. With long-time strength in cloud software and hardware, the firm is well-positioned to grow in the current cycle. The big question is, does the high debt burden throw a wrench in what was a sanguine growth trajectory?

At this valuation, I see the stock as a favorable risk/reward. It has secured customers such as OpenAI (OPENAI), xAI, Meta, Nvidia (NVDA), and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). It remains unknown how its ramping capex will translate into actual top-line growth, however, and its competitive positioning is still in flux. Keep in mind that its debt was secured at generally low costs, with rates broadly below the internal rates of return in long-term projects.

Then, at Oracle’s Analyst Day in October, the management team outlined a goal to hit $225 billion of revenue in FY 2030 and 28% of annual EPS growth now till then. Key will be growth in OCI, which may reach upwards of 74% of total net sales by the end of the decade.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to grow significantly over the years ahead. Per-share operating earnings may hit $8 in the out year, while revenue growth is also slated to pick up with momentum through FY 2028.

The sell side has also turned more upbeat about the stock, given a high of 28 earnings upgrades over the past 90 days, compared to just 8 downgrades. To be clear, profitability trends are not all favorable—free cash flow per share is now in the red at -$2.10.

Oracle: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts; EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my April analysis. If we assume normalized non-GAAP EPS of $8 (near the out-year consensus) and apply a 30x multiple, then shares should trade near $240. That's about 20% above the current price and a hike from my $143 price objective from early in the second quarter—before the blowout Q1 report.

The 30x P/E is justified, in my view, due to it implying about a 1.5 PEG ratio (assuming long-term EPS growth of a conservative 20%).

A Modest PEG Ratio At Today's Share Price

Key risks for Oracle include its weak cash position and reliance on debt financing. Free cash flow is negative, and the debt-to-equity ratio is concerningly high. We see that reflected in the more expensive CDS.

Furthermore, the onus is on the management team to be effective on AI capex—the market is waving a yellow flag on that front right now. Supply chain issues and the reliability of data center rollouts are other potential issues to monitor in the quarters to come.

At the macro level, an economic growth downturn would negatively impact overall enterprise spending.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q2, 2026 earnings date of Monday, December 8 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares about 20% to the cheap side from a fundamental perspective, ORCL’s technical situation points to a possible bounce. Notice in the chart below that shares tagged their December 2024 peak of $198 after a brutal Thursday-Friday selloff this week (as of this writing).

Shares dipped below their long-term 200-day moving average, but that trend-indicator line remains upward sloping, suggesting the bulls haven’t given up their grip just yet.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It’s now in technical oversold territory, a level it hit back at the April share-price low. I like that there’s a high amount of volume by price beginning in the high $100s, but there’s also now a significant congestion zone starting in the high $200s.

For now, $235 to $240 is resistance, while a downside gap lingers at $180.

ORCL: Shares Touch Critical Long-Term Support Amid Oversold Conditions

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on Oracle. I was bullish on the stock earlier in the year, and it has been on a wild ride since then. Now in a more than 40% drawdown, shares appear favorable from both a fundamental and technical standpoint.

data.title: Nebius Stock: With Great Volatility Comes Great Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) shares have been on an absolutely wild ride the last few weeks as market volatility has picked up and the AI sector in particular is looking vulnerable for the first time since the April tariff wars. Despite the selloff, I think NBIS remains the stock to own in the neocloud space, and I think the current valuation represents an attractive entry point for those with the stomach to withstand what comes next.

In my previous piece on NBIS, I discussed the company's full-stack approach to AI workloads and how, among all the neocloud operators including its public peers CoreWeave (CRWV) and IREN (IREN), Nebius is the only one with a long-term moat. The stock has declined 25% in the intervening weeks as the AI trade appears to be teetering. That article can be read here.

Just for some additional context for those that might have missed it (even though it's literally all anyone is talking about right now), the once invincible AI sector appears to have finally shown some cracks, as the last few weeks have seen major declines across companies large and small. Meta (META) is down 20% after revealing accelerating AI capex projections in its recent earnings, Microsoft (MSFT) is down more than 10% from pre-earnings highs, Nvidia (NVDA) is down about 13% from recent highs despite an earnings report Wednesday that many thought would lift the market, and the Big 3 neocloud operators have all been hammered.

CoreWeave sits at about half the value it boasted just 3 weeks ago, IREN is down more than 40%, and NBIS is down about 35% as concerns increase that reduced AI spending could run the taps dry on these companies. There has also been a rise in macroeconomic fears, as the Fed has signaled some reluctance to cut interest rates again in December due to the specter of inflation. Odds of a rate cut before the year ends have fallen from the mid 90% range a few weeks ago to around 20% this week, which has investors revising expectations for cheaper capital.

So, bad call on my part, right? While it's not ideal that NBIS has dropped since my Buy rating, to be fair:

  1. The stock has held up much better than its peers in that timeframe.
  2. I added Sell ratings on both CRWV (article here) and IREN (article here) before the selloff, so a pair of trade would have been profitable.

Though to be even more fair, I didn't explicitly recommend a pair of trade, so I shouldn't be trying to take credit for its profit potential. But what we can do is re-assess Nebius' prospects in the wake of the ongoing news cycle and the stock's new valuation. First, I think it's important to explore why, in my opinion, NBIS held up better than its two competitors.

Starting with CoreWeave, the crux of my Sell rating in the aforementioned article was that the company has no software moat to lock customers into its services long-term, which leaves it vulnerable to being crowded out by the hyperscalers in the event of a downturn in AI demand, and that it is accumulating debt at a rapid pace, which poses an existential threat if the AI capex spigot gets turned off. The company is now staring down the barrel of $10 billion+ in long-term debt, just $2 billion in cash, and an AI market that might be entering correction territory.

It's too early to tell if this will end up spiraling into a larger issue for CoreWeave, but investors are evidently pricing in some of that risk. Lower rates would also mitigate some of the debt impact by allowing the company to re-finance and access cheaper capital, but with odds of a December cut dropping, the market is punishing stocks with leveraged balance sheets.

Moving onto IREN, the company doesn't have a debt problem, but it does have a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) problem. While it ramps up capacity to satisfy a $9.7 billion deal with Microsoft, IREN remains almost completely reliant on Bitcoin for revenue and covering expenses. In fact, the company generated $233 million in Bitcoin mining revenue in its most recent quarter, compared to just $7.3 million for AI cloud. Of course, this is just the early innings of a data center pivot, but these revenues are still covering operating expenses while that transition occurs, so the price of Bitcoin remains an important factor. The cryptocurrency's sell-off over the last couple of weeks has combined with waning AI sentiment to put a large dent in IREN's valuation.

In contrast, Nebius was built from the ground up as a fully integrated platform for AI workloads, servicing companies as large as Microsoft or as small as a team of researchers while offering all manner of dev tools and software layers on top of the hardware. This is the moat that turns Nebius from just another bare-metal server provider into a long-term player in the industry. Hardware is a race to the bottom, considering every company with a handful of GPUs is an Nvidia Cloud Partner these days -- software has been and will continue to be the true lock-in. Nvidia's CUDA is a great example of this.

Because Nebius didn't start as a Bitcoin mining operation that decided to follow the money and become an AI company, like CoreWeave and IREN, its software stack is significantly more mature than anything the other two have to offer and gives the company staying power in a sector that will likely continue to get more and more crowded.

Another reason I'm bullish on NBIS is the company's approach to expand aggressively while not getting too far ahead of its balance sheet:

Much of this cash was generated by a convertible note and equity offering in the quarter and will go towards capex in the quarters ahead, but compare this to CoreWeave, which is already pushing a 5x debt-to-cash & equivalents ratio, and the right-sizing by Nebius is apparent.

For these reasons, I think Nebius has the best approach and I think it has the highest likelihood of achieving the goals set out in Q3:

The company is undertaking rapid capacity expansion, plans to achieve 2.5 GW, and is gunning for annual run-rate revenue of $8 billion at the midpoint by the end of 2026. If Nebius can achieve that run rate and the 19% EBITDA margin it touts, the stock is in for a substantial boost.

I just want to highlight another example of right sizing supply and demand here, as the company notes on the slide that it is sold out of available capacity. The danger that a demand shock becomes an existential threat increases significantly the further supply is built out in anticipation of demand -- Nebius expanding its footprint while also being cognizant of the risks of an AI slowdown are another bullish indicator, in my opinion.

Investor Takeaway

So, what are investors to do now with NBIS in freefall, the AI market as a whole looking dodgy, and the direction of the U.S. economy feeling uncertain? In times like these, I think it's wise to target quality stocks and buy at a discount with the intention to hold long-term through any potential near-term volatility. I don't know if the current $80 range is the bottom or if the stock will continue to fall over the next few months and quarters, but I do think the company has solid long-term fundamentals and will likely trade higher than its current price in a few years' time.

Unfortunately, it's hard to value these companies since they're all mostly pre-revenue and the economics haven't really been fleshed, but if the annual run rate and margins are to be believed, NBIS' current $20 billion valuation looks quite attractive.

Nebius has major customer contracts secured, a best-in-class software stack, a healthy balance sheet, superb management, and an attractive vision for the future of AI workloads. That said, I think investors with low-risk tolerance might want to stay away -- market volatility is increasing and NBIS has demonstrated a propensity for wild swings that aren't for the faint of heart. However, I think those with a multi-year investing horizon and who aren't afraid of some bumps and bucks along the way should consider adding this stock to their portfolios.

I am upgrading NBIS from a Buy to a Strong Buy.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: Copa Holdings Remains A 'Strong Buy' Despite Q3 Sell-Off
data.description:
Copa Holdings (CPA) is one of the few airlines, if not the only one, on which I have a "Strong Buy" rating. In my last report, I pointed out the significant undervaluation, and since then the stock has returned 31.7%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 25.9% gain. Following the Q3 2025 earnings report, the stock price has fallen 10.5%. In this report, I discuss the airline’s Q3 2025 earnings and update my price target.

Copa Airlines Margins Expand In Q3

Looking at the Q3 2025 results, it is somewhat surprising that Copa Airlines stock tanked post-earnings. We see that total revenues increased 6.8% to $913.1 million, while operating expenses increased only 2.9% to $700.8 million. Operating profit increased 22.2% to $212.3 million, with margins expanding from 20.3% to 23.2%. EBIT increased 19.8% to $202.7 million, and net profit increased 18.7% to $173.4 million, indicating net margins of 19% compared to 17.1% a year earlier.

Passenger revenues rose 5.2% against a capacity increase of 5.8%, meaning that passenger unit revenues declined slightly. Total revenues benefited mostly from cargo and mail revenues as well as other revenues, resulting in a total unit revenue increase of 1%. So, unit revenues weakened, and yield, which measures the actual fare per mile rather than the revenue per available unit capacity, dropped 2.6%. In essence, we see that revenue strength held up rather well, but pricing strength declined. Unit costs declined 2.7%, while unit costs excluding fuel declined 0.8%.

The company enjoyed strong demand in Latin America. The markets in Brazil and Argentina are strong, but for the latter market that also means that competition is increasing and the market is being oversupplied. In Brazil, currency stabilization is driving market recovery.

Why Did Copa Stock Decline?

The decline of Copa’s share prices was not solely because of the Q3 results. I believe it is mostly driven by the guidance:

"As for our 2025 outlook, we remain confident in our full-year performance. We are reaffirming our guidance and narrowing the operating margin range to the upper end, now expected between 22% and 23%, with a full-year capacity growth projected at approximately 8%. This outlook reflects a healthy demand environment in the region as well as our continued cost discipline. Our outlook is based on the following assumptions: a load factor of approximately 87%; RASM of approximately $0.112; ex-fuel CASM of approximately $0.058; and an all-in fuel price of $2.40 per gallon. Looking ahead to 2026, we preliminarily expect full-year ASM capacity growth in the range between 11% and 13%, with an ex-fuel CASM in the range of $0.057 to $0.058. To conclude, we remain confident that our proven business model, robust balance sheet, and disciplined execution provide a solid foundation to continue delivering consistent growth, strong financial results, and industry-leading margins."

The primary concern regarding the guidance is that capacity growth will accelerate compared to Q3, with 8% capacity expansion expected for the full year and 11% to 13% capacity expansion expected for next year. In combination with the yield and passenger unit revenue pressure, that may point at a challenge to keep top-line growth in line with capacity growth and most likely remove some margin upside even if unit costs may come down.

Copa Airlines Stock Remains Undervalued Against Airline Peers

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

If we look at Copa Holdings stock, we see that the company is undervalued against its median EV/EBITDA as well as the peer group. EBITDA is expected to grow 8.8% annually, but free cash flow is set to decline compared to the levels seen in 2024, driven by higher capital expenditures. Against the median EV/EBITDA for Copa, the stock price target that is fundamentally supported is $189.08, indicating 65% upside. That would be conservative, given that we apply no forward valuation and we take the lower out of the company median and peer group median.

If we value the stock according to the current EV/EBITDA, which is even lower than the already discounted company median multiple, we get a stock price target of $152.71, indicating 33% upside. I also believe that this is a more realistic price target supporting my "Strong Buy" rating.

If we look at the EBITDA margin development, we see that margins are expected to improve, but there is no year-on-year improvement expected after 2026. Perhaps that does somewhat show that the company is already managed rather well from a margin perspective. Free cash flow generation is expected to be uneven, and this is driven by the capital expenditure cycle that the company is going through now. Gross debt is set to come down, and the same holds for the cash position. However, the net debt leverage is looking strong with 0.9x expected this year and around 0.6x in the years ahead. So, Copa Airlines has the space to actually refinance debt maturities without leverage becoming alarming. While I do not consider airline stocks the best investments for dividends, we also note that the stock actually seems to offer an attractive yield and the potential to grow yield on cost.

Conclusion: Copa Remains Underappreciated By The Market

Copa Airlines’ stock price remains undervalued, and while I do understand market concerns about softer yields this year with higher capacity expansions next year, I do not believe the current low EV/EBITDA trading multiple is justified. As a result, I am maintaining my "Strong Buy" rating for Copa Airlines.

data.title: Microsoft: I'm Waiting For $450 Entry (Downgrade, Technical Analysis)
data.description:
MSFT Stock: The Knife Is Likely To Fall More

I last analyzed Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) stock on Oct 11 with an article entitled “Microsoft: Margins Will Hold Despite Higher CAPEX Outlook.” That article was triggered by the earnings report (ER) the company released for its FQ4 2025, with a particular focus on its projected CAPEX expenditures and margins. The analysis concluded with a buy rating for the stock.

Since then, there have been a few new catalysts surrounding MSFT stock. The top catalysts as I see are:

  • A) its recent ER for FQ1 2026 (which MSFT released on Oct 29) and
  • B) the new technical trading signals after these ER releases.

As one of the most popular tickers on the Seeking Alpha platform, the financial updates in the ER have been thoroughly dissected by many other SA authors. And I see no need for further analysis anymore. Overall, the market responded to the ER with mixed stock price movements, as seen in the following chart. With the recent release of Nvidia's (NVDA) Q3 ER, the correction deepened. All told, these developments have pull MSFT’s share price back from its 52-week high (of around $555) by 11.46% as of this writing.

This could be tempting for potential investors who are interested in this leading stock in the AI space. However, my goal of this article is to explain why I see large odds for the price correction to continue and go as deep as around the $450 to $440 level in the near term. As a steward company, MSFT share prices have demonstrated a relatively low volatility when compared with other AI battlefield stocks. But the current correction is still relatively minor by historical standard. As an example, the chart below shows MSFT’s price corrections off-peak in the past 5 years. As seen, the worst pullback was about 40%, and the company has suffered 20% or more pullbacks (highlighted by the green line) at least once a year since 2022. With its 52-week peak price of $555 before this correction, a pullback of 20% would bring its share prices to $445. Next, I will explain why I see large odds for this scenario.

MSFT Stock: $440 To $450 Is The Next Support Level

Examining MSFT’s technical trading chart, I see a few indicators for its correction to continue in the near-term, with large odds to the $440~$450 level. After the stock price peaked at $555 after the ER, MSFT’s share prices have been on an overall downward path as seen. The downward movement has now gone beyond several key support levels.

To start, the stock prices have dipped below and stayed below the 20-day moving average (currently around $510) since early November, as seen. Second, I want to draw your attention to the price-volume bar highlighted by the green rectangle. This rectangle represents the trading range that has attracted the largest volumes lately. As such, I consider this price range (with a lower bound of around $490) the next key support level after the 20-day MA. MSFT’s current stock prices have also fallen below this level.

As another indicator signaling continued correction, I also want to bring your attention to the Bollinger Bandwidth (BB) show in the next chart. As seen, the current stock price has also fallen below the lower bound of the BB. Moreover, as you can tell clearly from the chart, the BB of MSFT has been narrowing significantly since September 2025. At the beginning of August, the low and upper Bollinger Band was about $445 and $535, respectively, resulting in a BB width of ~$90. Now, the low and upper Bollinger Band have narrowed to about $490 and $534 respectively, resulting in a BB width of around $44 only, about half of the width observed in August. In my experience, such a narrow BB is unlikely to sustain for too long, especially with the undergoing concerns many investors begin to develop about the near-term dynamics on the AI front.

Given the above technical signals, I expect near-term traders will be looking for the next support level. And I see $440 to $450 to be a very likely range for several reasons. First, my simulations show that if the stock corrects to this range in the next 5 trading days, the RSI signal (currently at 31.3) would enter the oversold territory, indicating potential exhaustion of sellers. Second, the $440 to $450 range is also the previous highs observed in early 2025 (as illustrated by the double green lines). Previous highs (aka resistant levels) often turn into support levels in during a correction.

MSFT Stock: Valuation And Return Projections

As a third reason, if/when MSFT’s prices do correct ~$450, the stock will become attractive valued in my model, thus drawing fundamental-oriented investors in and bringing about larger buying pressure subsequently. In the recent 2~3 years (i.e., since the rise of AI), MSFT stock has been priced at an FWD P/E ratio in the range about 28x and 36x with an average of 31.76x as shown in the next chart. With the updated guidance and EPS revisions following its recent ER, the stock’s current FWD P/E ratio is around 29.8x, already below the average of 31.76x (although only by a relatively moderate margin of ~7%). If the price indeed further corrects to ~$450 in the near future, MSFT’s FWD P/E would become ~28x at that price, near the most attractive levels observed in recent years.

Such an entry P/E would also offer robust long-term return potential for fundamental-oriented investors when combined with MSFT’s robust ROCE (return on capital employed) and capital-light business model. To wit, the next chart shows my estimated ROI (return on investment) under this entry P/E. The mechanisms behind this chart was detailed in our earlier article, and only the end results are quoted here to stay on point:

  • The long-term ROI for a business owner is simply determined by two things: A) the price paid to buy the business and B) the quality of the business. More specifically, part A is determined by the owner’s earning yield (“OEY”) when we purchased the business. Part B is determined by the quality of the business and that is why ROCE (return on capital employed) is the horizontal axis in the chart.
  • The long-term earnings growth rate is estimated by the product of the ROCE and the Reinvestment Rate.
  • Finally, long-term ROI = valuation + quality = OEY + Growth Rate = OEY + ROCE*Reinvestment Rate.

At a stock price of $450 and a FWD P/E of 28, the OEY would be at least 3.5%. Here, I emphasize “at least” because MSFT owners’ earnings significantly exceed its account EPS thanks to its capital-light operations. MSFT’s ROCE has been averaging around 65% in recent years. In terms of growth reinvestment rate, I expect the long-term average to settle around 15% (even the rate is far higher in recent years given the aggressive investment in AI infrastructure). With these parameters, the long-term organic growth rate would be almost 10% (65% ROCE x 15% reinvestment rate = 9.75%). As such, I see large odds for total ROI to be in the mid-teens on an annual basis, as shown in the chart.

MSFT Stock: Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks, both in the upside and downside directions, are worth mentioning. As detailed in my last article, I expected its CAPEX to stay elevated in the near future. After reading the new FQ1 ER, I had to move up my CAPEX estimate even higher for the FY of 2026. I currently estimate its CAPEX budget to run as high as $140 billion for FY 2026. With the latest revenue guidance, my CAPEX estimate would represent about 43% of its FWD revenue. To better contextualize things, the chart below provides a historical statistics of Microsoft’s annual CAPEX to revenue ratio in the past 5 years between 2021 and 2025.

As seen, before 2023, the ratio remained relatively stable and low, hovering near a baseline of approximately 0.12. However, beginning in 2023 with the rise of AI, the company significantly ramped up its capital spending intensity. This upward trajectory accelerated sharply and reached its current peak of 22.9%. If my above CAPEX estimate materializes, the CAPEX/revenue ratio would approximately double in FY2026 compared to the 2025 level. On the negative side, these huge investments could cause near-term profitability pressure.

On the positive side and especially for long-term oriented investors, these investments could further widen Microsoft’s scale advantage. For example, MSFT Office software suite has about 150 million users. Growing AI and copilot support has large potential to drive monetization/conversion for its Office subscribes and accelerate profit growth.

To wrap up, this MSFT follow-up was triggered by its FQ1 2026 ER and also the technical trading indicators after the ER. After examining these changes, I see large odds for the ongoing price correction to continue and for its share price to touch the ~$450 level in the near future. If this occurs, it could offer a compelling entry point for both near-term traders and also fundamental-oriented investors.

data.title: Allot: Accelerating Revenue Growth And Potential Margin Expansion
data.description:
Introduction

Previously, I have rated Allot Ltd. (ALLT) a "Buy" as the company not only landed multiple strategic deals but also was on track to achieve profitability by the end of FY2025. Surprisingly, in the latest quarter, Allot has made tremendous improvements and achieved TTM profitability way earlier than expected.

In addition, the company continues to see strong traction in its revenue expansion efforts and has the possibility to further expand its margins. Therefore, I have upgraded my rating from a "Buy" to a "Strong Buy". In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should consider adding Allot to their portfolios.

Allot Accelerated Revenue Growth, Achieved Profitability and Raised Guidance

In the most recent quarter, ALLT's revenue surged by 13.64% to $26.4 million. During this period, ALLT has also made tremendous improvements to its profitability. Operating income margin increased from 0.93% in 3Q24 to 8.14% in 3Q25 due to a huge reduction in operating expenses.

As a percentage of revenues, total operating expenses fell to 63.31% from 71.36%; this represents significant improvement of more than 800 bps. Consequently, Allot posted a net income of $2.83 million with a net income margin of 10.69%, as compared to a net loss of -244 thousand in the same period last year.

Looking ahead, due to persisting strong performance, Allot's management has raised its FY2025 guidance to $100 million to $103 million. Similarly, for FY2025, Allot's management expects SECaaS revenue to exceed 60% y/y; in the prior quarter, ALLT's management expects SECaaS revenue to grow between 55% y/y and 60% y/y.

SECaaS Segment Will Continue To Drive Allot's Revenue Expansion In The Foreseeable Future

In line with our previous analysis, Allot's revenue growth is primarily driven by its strong performance in its SECaaS segment; on a year-on-year basis, SECaaS revenues have expanded by 55.3%. Today, the company's SECaaS segment accounts for 28% of the company's total revenue as compared to merely 18% in FY2024.

More importantly, Allot continues to see sustained momentum in its SECaaS segment. For example, Allot has recently launched a new cybersecurity solution called OffNetSecure. This solution allows telecom providers to extend cybersecurity protection to its consumers and subscribers even if they are on other networks.

According to Allot's management, the company has just gained its first customer for OffNetSecure; separately, Allot is looking at its existing customers to cross-sell this new product. Thus far, there are already many potential customers in the company's pipeline. In other words, investors should expect OffNetSecure to contribute substantially as it further gains sales traction.

Other cybersecurity products continue to make significant progress; the company continues to win new major customers, execute more projects, and grow its backlog substantially. As compared to 4Q24, deferred revenues have surged by 56.2%; this suggests that investors should expect ALLT's high revenue growth to sustain for the foreseeable future.

Allot Is Still In Its Early Stage of Profitability And May See Further Margin Expansion

It is important to note that while Allot has achieved profitability, it is still in its nascent stages. For the first time in the past 10 years, the company has managed to attain TTM profitability. More importantly, given that Allot will see substantial revenue growth in the foreseeable future, there is a strong possibility that the company's profitability will further improve, especially if it can maintain cost discipline.

Based on TTM data, Allot has an operating income margin and net income margin of 1.37% and 1.06%, respectively. Utilizing A10 Networks, Inc. (ATEN), a company that is highly similar to Allot, we can understand the potential of Allot's profitability. Based on TTM data, A10 has an operating income margin and net income margin of 17.85% and 17.78%.

Therefore, if Allot can continue to maintain its cost discipline and expand its revenue capacity, there is a strong possibility that we will see Allot's operating income margin and net income margin improving by more than 1600 bps in the near future. Currently, based on consensus estimates, market participants are expecting Allot to achieve a net income margin of 15.44%. If Allot can reach or exceed A10's profitability by FY2027, there is a huge possibility that we see Allot's share price surprise to the upside.

Valuation Analysis Indicates A Potential Upside of More Than 30%

From a relative valuation perspective, Allot can be considered to be trading at an extremely lofty price. Based on all the important valuation metrics, Allot is trading at a substantially higher multiple as compared to the sector median. For example, Allot is trading at a non-GAAP FWD P/E of 50.26x and FWD Price/Sales of 4.04x as compared to the sector median of 21.81x and 3.14x. From a Price/Cash flow perspective, Allot is trading at 28.86x as compared to the sector median of 17.58x. At the very least, Allot is considered to be 28.79% more expensive than its peers.

That being said, to further ascertain the valuation of Allot, I have used the following assumptions and variables:

1. Forward P/E of 28.35x. This multiple is based on the current sector median FWD GAAP P/E. Given that Allot has finally achieved profitability and continues to show signs of strong revenue expansion, I believe that applying an industry multiple will be fair to the company.

2. FY2027 EPS of $0.41 per share. This is based on the current consensus estimates. Given that the company will continue to see its growth and profitability improving, I believe that the company can not only meet the current consensus estimates but also potentially surprising towards the upside. In addition, FY2027 consensus EPS is chosen to reflect a more normalized profit level and the company's potential growth in the foreseeable future.

Based on the above assumptions and variables, Allot's share price should be worth $11.62 per share. Currently, Allot is trading at $8.67, indicating that there is a potential upside of 34% for investors to capture.

Closing Remarks & Investment Risks

Overall, Allot continues to present a unique opportunity for investors who are seeking exposure in the cybersecurity industry. Even though Allot has made tremendous progress, the company is still severely undervalued. In the foreseeable future, investors should expect Allot's revenue and profitability to further improve substantially. More importantly, after factoring in Allot's potential growth, the company's share price is still considered cheap and offers a potential upside of 34%.

That being said, there is growing evidence that cybersecurity budgets are tightening; as a percentage of total information technology budgets, cybersecurity budgets have fallen to 10.9% from 11.9%. Moreover, overall cybersecurity growth has fallen to 4% from 8% in the prior year. This is the slowest in the past five years. If this trend persists, there is a possibility that we see Allot experiencing a longer and more difficult sales cycle, affecting the company's growth prospects.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Nvidia Dips, Bitcoin Cracks, Retail Winners Emerge
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia earnings - strong results driven by data center growth, but shares slip as markets turn cautious (0:25). Bitcoin drops below $83K, crypto weakness spreads (1:10). Retail earnings: Home Depot vs. Lowe’s; Walmart vs. Target (1:50). Fintech & BNPL pressure (4:20). September jobs data: negative revisions revive recession worries; unemployment rises (5:45). Tariff rollbacks and mixed Fed signals swing December rate-cut odds sharply (7:15). Thanksgiving week preview (9:20).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha. Welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here, thank you.

Rena Sherbill: Great to have you. Talk to us about this week. What's top of mind for you?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, obviously, Nvidia (NVDA) earnings were the centerpiece. In terms of results, the company remains indomitable, 66% data center growth driving strong revenue growth, better than expected results.

However, the market is squeamish. Initially, you saw gains in Nvidia, but eventually finished lower in the day after its earnings release. I think that points to an overall just concern about the economic situation, the prospects of an AI bubble and just a move away from more speculative investments.

I think people are trying to rein in their vision of when certain investments might pay off. They're bringing in their time horizon a little bit. And I think that plays out especially in the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) market.

You see Bitcoin continuing to slide below $83,000 as we started this call. It's the first time since April it's lost about a third of its value from its high. You can see that play out in a lot of the Bitcoin related stocks.

You see (COIN) down 14%, Strategy (MSTR) down 16%, Circle (CRCL) down 16%, Robinhood (HOOD) down 12%. This is all over the last five days. So I think that's a clear sign that people are continuing to reconfigure their portfolios for a little bit more safety.

Rena Sherbill: And earnings wise, what else did you see?

Brian Stewart: Moving beyond Nvidia, it was a big week for retailers and a big week for pairs of retailers and the different reactions to them.

For example, Home Depot (HD) dropped about six percent after its earnings. It missed on the bottom line. It cut its guidance. It's got a cautious outlook due to the prospects for consumer spending, a soft housing market.

So in general, some signs that there's hope maybe in 2026 that the housing market will bounce back. That's really dependent on how the economy shapes up. So people are starting to build in some doubts about about that aspect.

Meanwhile, you saw Lowe's (LOW). It dropped in sympathy with Home Depot when Home Depot's earnings came out, but then bounced when its own earnings were released. It beat expectations better than expected guidance. So the guidance still showed some caution about the same kind of factors that Home Depot is looking for.

Also, Lowe's have been down about 20 % since September going into its earnings, so that bounce, but you do see the two stocks, the different kind of reaction to the earnings coming out. And I think it's just a play on Lowe's maybe executing a little better in a tough environment than Home Depot has been.

Turning to another set of paired stocks, you can see Walmart (WMT) was up 6% on its earnings. It's not far from its 52 week high. It's within five or 10% of its 52 week high at this point. It topped expectations thanks to strong e-commerce, strong international sales. So you see Walmart, people turning to value, you see strong execution from them.

On the other side of the coin, you see Target (TGT), which dropped after its earnings. Target had its third consecutive quarter of declining comparable store sales. Comparable store sales were down about 2.7% for the most recent quarter.

And so again, I think you're seeing an execution differential there. You're seeing Walmart execute much better. But I think you also see a difference in business plan. You see Walmart having a more of a value play, having more of its business focused on things like groceries and essentials. So you're seeing, I think, consumers step down in terms of the costs they're willing to absorb.

Then I think you're also just seeing Walmart benefiting from the scale that it has. I think even in the tariff situation that we're in, the uncertainty, I just think a company the size of Walmart has more leverage to keep things steady, even as the environment is a little bit shaky.

Rena Sherbill: And speaking to that environment, what are you seeing broadly speaking, economically?

Brian Stewart: So one interesting sector to look at is the buy now pay later fintech sector. Financial stocks that are more in sort of the speculative economy. Klarna (KLAR) reported earnings this past week. It's the first report since it came public. It dropped 9 % after earnings, continued to fall after that, now down about 16 % for the last five days.

It posted a loss and I think most damning, especially for the sector in general, the provision for credit losses climbed to 235 million. This is compared to 116 million a year ago.

So you're seeing companies start to show concern about delinquencies and consumers not being able to pay back. You see Affirm (AFRM), which is a competitor of Klarna, down 13 % in last five days.

And then outside of that particular sector, you also see companies like SoFi (SOFI) is down 10 % in last five days, PayPal (PYPL) is down 7%. So I think there's some concern that if consumer cracks, the first place we're going to see that happen is in these financial stocks that are based on consumer lending or interactions with the consumer, small businesses, things like that.

And so I think just as we're looking ahead, especially as economic data is starting to come back, on the horizon, I think that's a key place to look.

Rena Sherbill: And what is coming out of that economic data that we're seeing, that we're getting these drips and drabs of previous economic data releases that are just now coming out? What are you seeing there?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, so this week we saw the jobs data. This is the jobs data for September. it's a backward looking report, but it beat expectations on the headline number. So we added 119,000 jobs in September, much better than expected.

However, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%. This is largely due to people re-entering the job market. I think the most concerning aspect of the report was the revisions for previous months. So August is now showing a loss of 4,000 jobs, whereas before it was showing a gain of 22,000 jobs.

When you see the number cross that zero mark, so we actually had the hint of a recession in August with those job losses. Now, that could just be a one-time blip. It could come back, but it had been an extremely weak set of jobs report even going into the September report.

So September being better than expected really didn't do much to soothe concerns about the economy, especially since you're seeing a pattern of relatively strong reports and then revisions later. So if we see the September number get revised lower in the future, I think that'll be a meaningful step, especially as we wait for the backlog of economic data that's coming out.

Rena Sherbill: And we're seeing a rollback of some tariffs in the past couple of days. How does that play into this economic conversation or is that an obvious cause and effect? Like if people aren't doing that well, they're rolling back some tariffs.

Brian Stewart: Yeah. I think, it's coming at a good time, obviously. mean, to your point, it's not confusing why it's happening now. And, I mean, one thing, however you feel about the Trump administration, it is responsive to different political winds. So it'll throw something out, try it. If it doesn't work, there's a lot of pushback. They'll pull back, they'll reassess, move around.

The tariffs is the best example of that. There hasn't been a consistent tariff policy for more than a couple of weeks the entire time the tariff discussion has been going on. You can see the administration responding to the calls for affordability and you can see that play out in the Fed as well.

There was high expectation that there'd be a series of rate cuts coming. And then as the economic data started to come in and people began worried about affordability, became worried about inflation again, you see the Fed sort of pulling back.

And now we're in sort of a mixed situation with the Fed where expectations for what's going to happen in the December meeting are bouncing around a lot. So for instance, just in the last couple of days, yesterday, the market was pricing in a 40 % chance that the Fed would cut rates at the December meeting.

Today, it's not pricing in a 75% chance that that would happen. So it's actually flip-flopping in terms of probably won't happen, probably will happen. And the main news today is that the New York Fed President said there could be some room for further rate cuts. He's a pretty heavy voice among policymakers, and so people are putting a lot of weight on that.

However, if you look at the other comments that have come from Fed speakers in recent weeks, it's pretty mixed. Some think no cuts, some think there should be a cut. The minutes of the last Fed meeting show that there was a pretty sharp division, pretty hot debate among the policymakers of whether or not there should be a rate cut. So I really think you're going to see that number, the odds of a rate cut December flip-flop a lot between now and when the meeting takes place. I think it's going to be difficult to get any clarity on that.

Rena Sherbill: What else would you add to this week's conversation?

Brian Stewart: Looking forward, we have Thanksgiving next week, so the market is going to be closed on Thursday, half day Friday, there's going to be less volume just because people are out of the office. So you could see volatility increase in a condition like that.

So if some unexpected good or bad news comes out, you can see a little more dramatic move. And then next week is very, because of the holiday, all the news releases really pumped into the first three days. You're getting a bunch of September economic data that had been halted because of the government shutdown. You're gonna get a flood of that next week.

Plus there's a bunch of earnings. mean, nothing really dramatic on the earnings schedule, just a lot of, I'm trying to be polite, second tier companies. You have companies like Deere (DE), you have another set of retailers coming out, you have Dick's (DKS), Best Buy (BBY), Abercrombie and Fitch (ANF).

And so you're going to get a fair amount of information, especially in the first three days, and then you're going to have lower volume. I just think it sets the stage and the market's already jittery. And so it sets the stage for a situation where I think the market's likely going to stay in a wait and see kind of attitude. So expect it to bounce around and feel things out for whatever reason.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah, patience is certainly key right now, maybe more than ever. It's certainly key right now in terms of the upcoming week and we'll be off next week. So we will talk to you again in a couple of weeks, but any sense of Black Friday and what that may portend?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, that's a good point, especially on the subject of retailers. I think we're going to get some data, we're going to hear the data about that the week after. So I do think it's a crucial data point as we go into the holiday season, especially on two fronts. Retailers specifically, how well they're going to close out the year when you're looking at the Target, Walmart divide in retailers where it's really been a stock pickers market in retailers lately.

And then the second aspect of Black Friday is just the overall economic information that we're going to get from it. So how is the consumer doing? Are they spending? Are they trying to find deals?

Are they trying to move up to luxury brands just because, maybe in an inflationary environment where mid-tier things are more expensive than you expect, maybe there's some value in just going whole hog and buying luxury items, things of that nature. So we're gonna get some information.

Rena Sherbill: Well, happy Thanksgiving to everybody. Happy Thanksgiving, Brian. Gratitude is a great attitude to have, to keep in mind. Talk to you soon.

Brian Stewart: Talk to you soon.
